93GW-CZW: An edge-tool maker from Montreuil-Bellay in France's Loire valley, Guillaume sailed aboard the Saint-Jehan in 1636 with his wife Françoise, two young daughters, and a valet, becoming one of the first French families to settle in Acadia. Recorded as an officer of cavalry, he raised a large family at Port-Royal, where a 1678 census shows him farming four acres with cattle and many children. He lived there until his death around 1682.
9K9K-2VH: Baptized at Clerkenwell in London in 1730, Thomas crossed the Atlantic and made his home in the North Carolina tidewater, settling among the plantations of Halifax and Edgecombe counties. He spent his later years there, and his estate passed through probate at Halifax in 1790. Though few details of his life survive, he was part of the steady English migration that peopled colonial North Carolina.
G32F-NF4: Born in the village of Niederlinxweiler in the German Saarland in 1721, Johann Windel Müller joined the wave of German settlers who pushed onto the Virginia frontier. He carved out a homestead along Patterson Creek in the rugged country of present-day West Virginia. His life ended violently in August 1761, when he was killed in an Indian attack during the dangerous frontier years of the French and Indian War.
GHZN-HBZ: A native of St. Clement in Cornwall, John Ambrose Parks came to Virginia as a young man, with records placing his arrival in the 1650s. He put down roots on Virginia's Eastern Shore in Accomack County, where he was known as a captain and lived out a long life. He died there in 1709 after more than half a century in the colony.
KCS5-KYJ: Born in the north of England around 1690, Thomas Lamb belonged to a Quaker family of the Society of Friends. He immigrated to British colonial America in 1729 and settled in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, where he married Mary Webster, daughter of a local Quaker family. He raised his family among the close-knit Friends community before his death in 1752.
KNC6-52T: Sarah Andrews was born in Hartford, Connecticut, in 1680, during the early generations of that colony's settlement. She lived her life in Connecticut and died at Ridgefield in Fairfield County in 1719. Little documentation of her story survives beyond the bare record of a life lived in the early Connecticut towns.
KZX1-K2H: Born in France around 1615, Andrée Guyon became the ancestral matriarch of the Acadian Belliveau family. After an early marriage in France to a man named Bernard, during which she lost two infant children, she crossed to the New World and around 1651 married Antoine Belliveau. The 1671 census of Port-Royal records the couple farming with eleven cattle and eight sheep alongside their children. She lived to about seventy, last noted in the census of 1686.
L2QV-T53: John David Ross, Jr. was born in Angus, Scotland, in 1696. He emigrated to the Virginia colony around 1716, apparently making the crossing alone after his father's death rather than traveling as a family. He settled in St. Paul's Parish in Hanover County, Virginia, where he lived until his death in 1759.
L4JV-6P4: A son of Maryland's prominent Hammond family, Thomas was born in Anne Arundel County around 1668 and rose to lead a busy public life. He served as Captain of Horse and Commander of Baltimore County, climbing the militia ranks to Colonel, and sat in the Maryland Assembly representing both Anne Arundel and Baltimore counties over many years. A justice and county commissioner who helped lay out new towns, he died in 1724 at his estate "Heath."
L63K-CND: Born in Bristol, England, in 1642, Symon Miller worked as a bookseller before crossing to Virginia. He settled along the Rappahannock River in Essex County, where he was known as a captain and lived through the turbulent years of Bacon's Rebellion. He drew up his will in 1679 and died in 1684, leaving behind a name that appeared variously as Symon and Simon in the records.
L7YK-SN6: Born in Orange County, North Carolina, in 1795, Abraham Whinery became one of northwest Arkansas's most famous pioneers. After moving through Tennessee, he settled in early Benton County, Arkansas, where he served as soldier, statesman, and civic leader. He rose to the rank of General, sat in the state legislature, and helped found his community as a church trustee. He died in Benton County in 1866, remembered as one of its most important founding citizens.
LBJ4-Z5L: Born in 1618 in the village of Tinsley in Yorkshire, England, Thomas Tinsley sailed for the colonies as a young man around 1638. He made his life in Virginia, settling in St. Peter's Parish in New Kent County and raising two sons. He lived to the remarkable age of eighty-four, dying in New Kent in 1702 after more than six decades in the New World.
LCBW-D5H: Born in Lancaster, Pennsylvania, in 1745, James Edgar Gilliland made his way south to the Virginia frontier and settled in Botetourt County. He was remembered as a lieutenant whose family carried a tradition of military service. He died in Botetourt in 1810, his life rooted in the backcountry of colonial and early-republic Virginia.
LCZR-WSL: Born in 1658 along Virginia's Rappahannock River, John Catlett Jr. became one of Essex County's leading gentlemen. He served as Justice of the Peace, Coroner, and Sheriff, and represented Essex in the House of Burgesses through the 1690s and into the 1700s. A militia colonel and substantial landholder who patented hundreds of acres and helped lay out the town of Rappahannock, he died in 1724, leaving a will that named a large family.
LH7P-QMB: Born in Anson County, North Carolina, in 1737, Enoch B. Floyd descended from a family that had emigrated from England and Wales in the 1600s. He served as a soldier in the Revolutionary War and afterward settled in the South Carolina upcountry, with records placing him in the Spartanburg district in 1790. He died in Union County, South Carolina, in 1795.
LHWG-RY6: Born in Plymouth, Devon, in 1626, Matthias Nicoll came to America and rose to prominence in colonial New York. He served as Secretary of the colony, sat on the council, and was Mayor of New York City in 1672, later serving as a justice of the assizes and a judge. He settled at Plandome Manor on Long Island, where he died in 1687, founding a family that figured prominently in New York affairs.
LK56-1P1: Judge George Aston Sr. was born in Birmingham, England, in 1671. He crossed to British colonial America and settled in Chester, Pennsylvania, where he was remembered as a judge. He died there in 1728. Little else of his story survives in the family record.
LRB5-HFB: Born at Hampton, New Hampshire, in 1650, Isaac Chase was the progenitor of the Chase family of Martha's Vineyard. A blacksmith of Quaker faith, he came to Tisbury to settle in 1674, though the townsmen at first refused him liberty to live there, likely because of his religion. He overcame their objection and made his home on the island, where he lived until his death in 1727.
LT5R-K32: A wheelwright from Rayleigh in Essex, England, John Stockbridge sailed from London aboard the Blessing in 1635 with his wife Anne and young son, arriving in New England that summer. He settled at Scituate in Plymouth Colony, where he became known less for piety than for his outspoken nature, being fined for "disgraceful speeches" against the colony's government. He later removed to Boston, where he died in 1657.
LVC1-SGD: Born in Reading, Berkshire, in 1640, Joseph Phipps suffered imprisonment in his hometown as a Quaker dissenter before emigrating to Pennsylvania in 1682. He quickly took a place in public life, serving as an assemblyman for Chester in 1683. He settled at Abington near Philadelphia, where he drew up his will in 1709 and died in 1716.
LX39-ZC9: Born in St. Peter's Parish, New Kent County, Virginia, in 1677, William McGehee descended from a family of Scottish origin that had emigrated to King William County. A Quaker by faith, he held land in Virginia and beyond and lived in the King William and Caroline County country. He drew up his will at St. John's Parish in 1727 and died in Caroline County around 1748.
LY7V-XZN: Born in 1735 in London Grove Township, Chester County, Pennsylvania, James Lindley moved south as a young man, settling first in the North Carolina backcountry and by 1767 in South Carolina, where he accumulated substantial land. When the Revolution divided the frontier, he took the Loyalist side, serving as a captain in the South Carolina Royalists. Captured by the Patriots, he was tried for treason and hanged at Ninety-Six in April 1779.
LZ68-L3R: Born in Paris around 1700, Anne Françoise Roland came from a troubled household and as a rebellious teenager was jailed in the infamous La Salpêtrière prison on charges of debauchery. In 1719 she was among the women shipped from France to populate the new colony of Louisiana. She made a fresh life along the Mississippi, settling at Pointe Coupée, where she died in 1758.
LZFW-WKG: Mary Hunt was born in Gretton, Northamptonshire, England, in 1620. She immigrated to British colonial America in the 1630s and settled in Isle of Wight County, Virginia, where she lived out her life and raised a family. She died around 1685, part of the early English migration that built Virginia's tidewater communities.
LZR7-RMP: Born in Oxford, England, around 1630, John Buckner crossed to Virginia and made his home at Marlfield in Gloucester County, working as a salter. As clerk of the Gloucester County court, his name is tied to a notable episode in 1682, when his indentured servant William Nuthead printed the colony's acts of assembly without a license, prompting the governor's council to demand a bond against further unlicensed printing. He died in 1695.
MCV3-FT1: Born at Bristol in 1630, Walter Chiles II came to Virginia in 1638 aboard a ship his own father had fitted out for the family. A merchant who rose high in colonial affairs, he served repeatedly as a burgess, sat on the Governor's Council, and in 1653 was elected the eighth Speaker of the Virginia House of Burgesses. He also served as a justice of James City County and held the rank of lieutenant colonel before his death in 1671.
MQ8P-J73: Born in Virginia around 1670, Cornelius Vaughan was an active frontier landholder in the colony's tidewater counties. In 1696 he and a partner were granted 450 acres in King and Queen County for transporting nine settlers to Virginia, and he steadily added to his holdings through deeds along Beverley's Swamp and in Spotsylvania County. He died in 1735, leaving a will that named many children, including one yet unborn, and a wife, Elizabeth.
